APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses semantic change and cross-context lexical analysis—clearly within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue and targeted to the right audience, using standard datasets (COHA) and comparing to established baselines (Hamilton et al., Kim et al., Kulkarni et al.) with multiple embedding methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS). However, from my perspective as a theory-oriented reviewer there are minor scope concerns: the work is largely empirical and lacks formal problem formulation, theoretical guarantees, or discussion of failure modes, and code/variance/statistical testing are not mentioned. Those omissions do not exclude it from the venue but make it a slightly imperfect fit without stronger theoretical framing or reproducibility details. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP venues: it proposes a general-purpose framework (CCLA) for cross-context lexical analysis and evaluates it on semantic change, comparative lexical analysis, and embedding stability—topics of core interest to the community. The methodology and evaluation meet community expectations by comparing PPMI, SVD, and SGNS annotations and against established baselines (Hamilton et al., Kim et al., Kulkarni et al.), but empirical breadth is somewhat limited by a single corpus (COHA), which raises minor scope concerns about generality. Overall it addresses the right audience and uses appropriate formulations and evaluation standards, with practical value for researchers and practitioners.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses semantic change and cross-context lexical analysis—clearly within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue and targeted to the right audience, using standard datasets (COHA) and comparing to established baselines (Hamilton et al., Kim et al., Kulkarni et al.) with multiple embedding methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS). However, from my perspective as a theory-oriented reviewer there are minor scope concerns: the work is largely empirical and lacks formal problem formulation, theoretical guarantees, or discussion of failure modes, and code/variance/statistical testing are not mentioned. Those omissions do not exclude it from the venue but make it a slightly imperfect fit without stronger theoretical framing or reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP venues: it proposes a general-purpose framework (CCLA) for cross-context lexical analysis and evaluates it on semantic change, comparative lexical analysis, and embedding stability—topics of core interest to the community. The methodology and evaluation meet community expectations by comparing PPMI, SVD, and SGNS annotations and against established baselines (Hamilton et al., Kim et al., Kulkarni et al.), but empirical breadth is somewhat limited by a single corpus (COHA), which raises minor scope concerns about generality. Overall it addresses the right audience and uses appropriate formulations and evaluation standards, with practical value for researchers and practitioners.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the principal prior works in diachronic word meaning (Hamilton et al. 2016; Kim et al. 2014; Kulkarni et al. 2015) and evaluates multiple annotation schemes (PPMI, SVD, SGNS) on the same COHA data, so baseline choice is appropriate. However, from the summary it is unclear whether all evaluations use identical metrics and statistical reliability estimates (e.g., variance over seeds or significance tests) or whether the paper reports when prior methods match or outperform CCLA; these omissions weaken claims of fairness and thoroughness. The authors do clearly articulate how CCLA differs (NN-list based scoring avoiding matrix alignment) and the related-work coverage is described as thorough, so I deduct only minor points for missing robustness/acknowledgement details. | B(4): The paper compares against the main, relevant baselines (Hamilton et al. 2016; Kim et al. 2014; Kulkarni et al. 2015) and evaluates across multiple annotation methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS) on the same corpus (COHA), which matches my emphasis on empirical breadth and practical baselines. It clearly differentiates its nearest-neighbor/list-based scoring from alignment-based approaches and reports results across comparable conditions, but the summary does not state whether prior methods are explicitly acknowledged when they match or exceed CCLA’s performance nor give full detail on metric parity, so I withhold a perfect score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the principal prior works in diachronic word meaning (Hamilton et al. 2016; Kim et al. 2014; Kulkarni et al. 2015) and evaluates multiple annotation schemes (PPMI, SVD, SGNS) on the same COHA data, so baseline choice is appropriate. However, from the summary it is unclear whether all evaluations use identical metrics and statistical reliability estimates (e.g., variance over seeds or significance tests) or whether the paper reports when prior methods match or outperform CCLA; these omissions weaken claims of fairness and thoroughness. The authors do clearly articulate how CCLA differs (NN-list based scoring avoiding matrix alignment) and the related-work coverage is described as thorough, so I deduct only minor points for missing robustness/acknowledgement details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against the main, relevant baselines (Hamilton et al. 2016; Kim et al. 2014; Kulkarni et al. 2015) and evaluates across multiple annotation methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS) on the same corpus (COHA), which matches my emphasis on empirical breadth and practical baselines. It clearly differentiates its nearest-neighbor/list-based scoring from alignment-based approaches and reports results across comparable conditions, but the summary does not state whether prior methods are explicitly acknowledged when they match or exceed CCLA’s performance nor give full detail on metric parity, so I withhold a perfect score.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the method (CCLA) and its applications are presented early with sufficient procedural detail (NN lists, φ scoring, derived φ′), and hyperparameters and related work are reported thoroughly. Notation and experiments (PPMI/SVD/SGNS on COHA) appear consistent and examples/figures are informative, making the approach easy to follow. From a theory-purist perspective it would benefit from an explicit statement of limitations and failure modes and clearer signposting of assumptions, but these are presentation/coverage issues rather than structural clarity problems. | B(4): Overall the paper reads well and appears well-organized from intro through methods to experiments and conclusion, with main contributions (CCLA framework and its three applications) stated early. Notation and methodology (nearest-neighbor lists, φ score, φ′ derivation) are presented clearly and consistently, and evaluations across PPMI, SVD, and SGNS provide practical breadth that aids understanding. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though the absence of stated limitations and no mention of code release are minor presentation gaps that could be made explicit for greater reproducibility and clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the method (CCLA) and its applications are presented early with sufficient procedural detail (NN lists, φ scoring, derived φ′), and hyperparameters and related work are reported thoroughly. Notation and experiments (PPMI/SVD/SGNS on COHA) appear consistent and examples/figures are informative, making the approach easy to follow. From a theory-purist perspective it would benefit from an explicit statement of limitations and failure modes and clearer signposting of assumptions, but these are presentation/coverage issues rather than structural clarity problems.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper reads well and appears well-organized from intro through methods to experiments and conclusion, with main contributions (CCLA framework and its three applications) stated early. Notation and methodology (nearest-neighbor lists, φ score, φ′ derivation) are presented clearly and consistently, and evaluations across PPMI, SVD, and SGNS provide practical breadth that aids understanding. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though the absence of stated limitations and no mention of code release are minor presentation gaps that could be made explicit for greater reproducibility and clarity.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper introduces a practical, useful CCLA framework that avoids costly alignment and demonstrates broad empirical applications, which is a valuable engineering contribution. However, they also agree the core idea is an incremental methodological heuristic rather than a fundamentally new theoretical advance and lacks formal guarantees. Given this balance—useful and empirically informative but not highly original—I assign a middling originality score of 3.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper packages a useful, general framework (CCLA) that sidesteps embedding alignment by comparing within-context nearest-neighbor lists, which is a pragmatic and somewhat novel operationalization compared to alignment-heavy prior work (Hamilton et al., Kim et al., Kulkarni et al.). However, the core technical move—using NN-list similarity of annotated vectors (PPMI, SVD, SGNS)—is a relatively simple, incremental idea rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm, and no formal properties or theoretical guarantees are provided to elevate it beyond an empirical heuristic. The breadth of applications (diachronic change, metadata comparisons, stability) is informative, but an expert would view this as a notable engineering/generalization effort rather than a highly original theoretical contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable, practically useful idea: a general CCLA framework that compares context-specific lexical representations via nearest-neighbor list similarity, avoiding costly matrix alignment. This is not a trivial reapplication of existing alignment-based semantic-change methods (e.g., Hamilton et al. 2016); the NN-list scoring and derived φ′ for context representativeness are a clear methodological departure and useful heuristic. The work’s breadth—evaluating PPMI, SVD, and SGNS on COHA and applying the framework to diachronic change, metadata-based comparison, and stability analysis—adds empirical novelty and practical value that would inform experts, even if it builds on well-studied problems.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides thorough empirical evaluation across datasets, annotation methods, and relevant baselines and reports hyperparameters, which gives solid empirical support for the method. They also concur the work lacks ablation/sensitivity analyses, reporting of statistical variance/significance, discussion of failure modes/limitations, and explicit code release, which prevents it from being fully convincing. Given the strong experimental breadth but important missing analyses, a score of 4 reflects its practical usefulness while noting the methodological gaps to address.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a coherent method (CCLA), evaluates it on a canonical corpus (COHA) with multiple annotation methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS) and compares to relevant baselines, which provides reasonable empirical support for the core claim. However, there is no mention of ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g. varying k, embedding dimensionality) nor of statistical variance/significance over random seeds, and no discussion of failure modes or limitations—issues my reviewer persona treats as essential for a methodological contribution. Reproducibility is only partially addressed (hyperparameters reported) but code availability is not mentioned, which constrains exact replication.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents reasonably comprehensive experiments: CCLA is evaluated on multiple tasks (diachronic change, metadata-based comparison, stability) using COHA and across several annotation methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS), and it compares to relevant baselines (Hamilton et al., Kim et al., Kulkarni et al.). Related work coverage is reported as thorough and hyperparameters are given, aiding reproducibility; however code availability is not mentioned. The paper lacks explicit ablation/sensitivity analyses and does not discuss failure cases or limitations, so while empirically solid and practically useful, it misses important analyses that would make the evidence fully convincing.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree CCLA is a practical, broadly applicable, alignment-free method that will be useful to practitioners and is demonstrated robustly across annotation methods and COHA. The lower concerns—absence of formal guarantees, ablation/statistical robustness analyses, and missing stated limitations or code—limit its theoretical transformative impact, but do not outweigh its clear practical utility and likelihood of adoption, so a score of 4 reflects strong applied impact with some caveats.

reviewer_a.justification: CCLA frames a broadly applicable practical approach to cross-context lexical analysis that sidesteps embedding alignment and is demonstrated across multiple annotation methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS) on COHA, so it is likely to be adopted as a useful tool. However, from a theory-first perspective its impact is limited by a lack of formal guarantees, ablation/statistical robustness analyses, and no stated limitations or code release in the summary; these omissions reduce confidence that it will change core theoretical thinking. The method could open useful applied directions in semantic change and stability assessment but appears more of a pragmatic contribution than a field-transforming one.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—comparing lexical meaning across arbitrary corpus partitions—by proposing CCLA, a general, alignment-free framework that is directly useful for diachronic analysis, metadata-based comparisons, and measuring representation stability. I value its empirical breadth: evaluations on COHA and use of multiple annotation methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS) demonstrate robustness and make the approach readily adoptable by practitioners. While not overturning theoretical foundations, CCLA provides a versatile, reproducible procedure that will change how researchers perform cross-context lexical comparisons and is likely to be widely built upon in applied work.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a clear, technically plausible framework with broad empirical comparisons across annotation methods, and the core NN-list approach appears correct. They also concur that important methodological rigor is missing—no significance testing, variance/robustness analyses, ablations, theoretical guarantees, or explicit code release—which weakens confidence in generality and reproducibility. Given the strong empirical breadth and sound technical idea but notable gaps in experimental rigor, a score of 4 reflects solid work that would benefit from additional robustness and reproducibility analyses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (CCLA as a general framework) and supports it with experiments on COHA and comparisons to relevant baselines (Hamilton et al., Kim et al., Kulkarni et al.) using multiple annotation methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS). However, from the summary there are no formal theoretical results or proofs about the properties of the proposed nearest-neighbor list similarity measure, and key methodological rigor is missing: I see no mention of statistical significance testing, variance estimates over random seeds or folds, nor detailed ablation studies quantifying each component's contribution. These omissions leave open questions about robustness and whether the empirical findings generalize beyond the reported settings, so while promising, the technical evidence is incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a clear, technically plausible framework (CCLA) and supports its main claims with experiments across multiple annotation methods (PPMI, SVD, SGNS) on COHA and comparisons to established baselines (Hamilton et al., Kim et al., Kulkarni et al.). Mathematical approach (NN-list similarity avoiding embedding alignment) is straightforward and appears correct given the summary, and empirical breadth is a strong point. However, the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing, explicit code release or robustness analyses, and no limitations are discussed—issues that are minor but leave some empirical reproducibility and confidence questions. Overall the technical work is solid with only modest gaps in experimental rigor and reproducibility.

main_claims: Proposes CCLA, a general framework for cross-context lexical analysis that compares term representations across arbitrary context partitions of a corpus.
Applies CCLA to three tasks: semantic change detection (diachronic analysis), comparative lexical analysis across metadata (e.g., sentiment), and comparison of word representation stability.
Introduces a similarity scoring approach that compares nearest-neighbor lists derived from within-context word vector annotations, avoiding the need to align embedding matrices across contexts.
Demonstrates CCLA on COHA (1900 vs 1990) using PPMI, SVD, and SGNS annotations and shows it can recover known semantic shifts and measure embedding stability.
_summary.methodology: Represent each word in a context by its d-dimensional word vector, compute its top-k nearest-neighbor lists per context, and score words by the cosine similarity between these NN lists (φ); use head/tail of the score distribution to find stable/changed words and a derived score φ′(C1,CB)=φ(C1,CB)−φ(C1,C2) to find context-representative words.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic change / cross-context lexical analysis

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a track record of formal models for distributional semantics and representation learning.
traits: penalises papers that present predominantly empirical heuristics without formal definitions, theorems, or provable properties
requires clear baseline comparisons and ablation studies quantifying contributions of each component
deducts points when statistical significance tests or variance estimates (e.g., over random seeds or folds) are missing
prefers methods with explicit assumptions about data-generating processes and penalises methods that lack discussion of failure modes or theoretical limitations
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on methodological rigor and theoretical grounding; skeptical of empirical-only contributions unless they provide clear, reproducible, and thoroughly ablated evidence.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Innovator
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher in industry-academia boundary with experience deploying lexical-semantic tools for diachronic analysis, social media monitoring, and lexicon stability assessments.
traits: rewards novel, general-purpose frameworks that can be applied across multiple settings or tasks
awards extra credit for empirical breadth: evaluations across several annotation methods (e.g., PPMI, SVD, SGNS) and realistic corpora
values reproducibility and practical guidance (e.g., code release, clear hyperparameters, simple heuristics that work well) and upgrades papers that demonstrate usable procedures
tolerant of heuristic approaches if they are empirically robust, interpretable, and enable new analyses for practitioners
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes practical impact and empirical breadth; favors clear, usable methods that open up new directions even if they lack deep theoretical analysis.
